video_title: Abundance vs Scarcity: We Built 20 Brands With 30 Strangers In 90 Days | The Foundery
gist: Nikhil Kamath, Kishore Biyani (KB) and Ronnie Screwvala audit 'The Foundery', a 90-day residential incubator in Alibaug where 25 founders each get ~4 crore and a year of pre-built groundwork to launch new-age Indian consumer brands, while the Foundery keeps ~75% equity. The conversation lays out a thesis that India's path to a $10T consumption economy runs through innovative, brand/design/consumer-first FMCG products for the affluent 'India One' (~30M families), and debates whether abundance or scarcity builds better founders.

insights.claim: The Foundery gives each of ~25 founders roughly 4 crore rupees and a 90-day residential program in Alibaug to build a new-age consumer brand, with the Foundery retaining ~75% of equity and the founder keeping ~25%.
insights.speaker: Nikhil Kamath
insights.so_what: A novel parallel-incubation structure that turns brand-building into a portfolio bet; investors watching India consumer should track whether high-equity-retention studio models can out-perform traditional founder-led startups.
insights.quote: if they were to start a company in Bangalore or elsewhere here they get to keep 25% of equity whereas the Foundery keeps 75%

insights.claim: Screvala defends the 75/25 split by arguing a founder would lose 40-60% of equity anyway raising early money and paying for co-founders, so a year of pre-built incubation that saves roughly two years justifies the Foundery's larger stake.
insights.speaker: Ronnie Screwvala
insights.so_what: Frames the equity discount as time-arbitrage; the bet is that compressed time-to-market and de-risked ideas are worth more than the diluted ownership stake.
insights.quote: You would have lost 40%, 50%, 60% of your company raising money... and anyway be at about 25% in my view to get to that same place

insights.claim: KB argues founder value comes from absolute outcome not equity percentage, citing that a 25% stake in a $2B company beats a larger stake in a $1B company, and that the Foundery offers a faster, more competitive path to scale.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: The studio's pitch rests on raising expected exit value enough to offset dilution; investors should test whether Foundery brands actually reach materially higher valuations than independent peers.
insights.quote: the 25% at the end of the day is the value you create. It's not the percentage

insights.claim: The Foundery spends approximately one crore rupees per founder on the program over and above the capital committed to each business.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the studio's per-head overhead, a key input for assessing the economics and break-even of the incubator model at scale.
insights.quote: we are spending one crore on every person in this programme

insights.claim: The Foundery generated roughly 100,000 applicants from minimal announcements, which the founders treat as evidence of latent entrepreneurial demand and a marketing moat in itself.
insights.speaker: Santosh
insights.so_what: A 100k-applicant funnel signals strong deal-flow and brand pull for a studio model; recurring batches could compound this top-of-funnel advantage.
insights.quote: you've got about 100,000 people without any mystifying, just on putting out a little bit of announcements

insights.claim: The program is structured as a 12-week curriculum where each week targets a goalpost (formulation, packaging, supply chain, distribution), with the 90th day being a 'gate' where founders present a product and business plan to invited investors.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: A staged, gated build process is designed to force speed-to-first-invoice; the demo day functions as a synchronized fundraising event for the whole cohort.
insights.quote: the program has been designed for a 12-week... every week with a subject. So that at the end of that week, you reach certain goalposts

insights.claim: The 90th day is an invite-only demo day for a small set of top VCs and family offices where all 20 brands are showcased with individual storytelling narratives rather than standard pitch decks.
insights.speaker: Bilal
insights.so_what: A curated, production-heavy demo day concentrates investor attention and could become a recurring annual deal-flow IP for Indian consumer venture.
insights.quote: It's going to be an invite-only event where we will get the best investors, the right kind of VCs, the right family offices

insights.claim: The founders frame the Foundery as long-term, non-exit capital and explicitly reject VC-style thinking, positioning themselves as founders first rather than investors.
insights.speaker: Ronnie Screwvala
insights.so_what: Patient capital with no exit pressure changes how these brands will be managed; counterparties and co-investors should expect longer holding horizons and lower fire-sale risk.
insights.quote: I don't think any of us are looking for exits per se

insights.claim: KB explicitly compares the Foundery's end-state to a Warren Buffett-style holding company that owns majority stakes in 20+ businesses and adds value, framing it as an FMCG conglomerate or holding-company wealth-creation vehicle.
insights.speaker: Nikhil Kamath
insights.so_what: Positions the Foundery as a permanent FMCG holding co rather than a one-off accelerator; the value-creation thesis hinges on majority ownership plus operational uplift across the portfolio.
insights.quote: Well, he's got majority stakes in multiple businesses and then worked with them and added value... So you can look at it like an FMCG. You can look like a conglomerate. You can look like a holding company.

insights.claim: KB argues the Foundery's success rate will be far higher than typical startups because the business ideas originate from the Foundery's consumer research, reducing the pivots a normal startup goes through.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: If idea-origination from proprietary consumer research genuinely lowers failure rates, the studio model could justify its equity take; the claim is testable against the cohort's survival rate.
insights.quote: our success rate will be far, far higher than any start-up which will be. Because it's coming with a lot of wisdom

insights.claim: The founders are betting India is heading toward a $10 trillion consumption economy and that the winning consumer brands will be built domestically rather than imported.
insights.speaker: Nikhil Kamath
insights.so_what: Anchors the entire Foundery thesis on domestic consumption scale; a core macro call for anyone allocating to Indian consumer brands.
insights.quote: India is heading towards a 10 trillion dollar consumption economy. The brands that win will be built here.

insights.claim: The Foundery targets 'India One' — defined as anyone with domestic help who doesn't do manual work themselves — estimated at around 30 million families, building mass-premium and premium products for them.
insights.speaker: KB / Nikhil Kamath
insights.so_what: A concrete 30M-family TAM definition for premium Indian consumers; useful sizing benchmark for consumer brand allocators.
insights.quote: Anybody who has domestic help at home and who doesn't do something with their own hands is India One

insights.claim: KB argues Indian consumers are not rejecting large corporations for small brands per se, but are gravitating toward Indian-made brands and innovative new products across categories.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the popular 'David vs Goliath' framing — the real driver is Indian-origin innovation, not anti-corporate sentiment; reframes the competitive thesis.
insights.quote: I'm seeing a trend about people liking Indian brands... They are experimenting with a lot of new ideas, a lot of new brands

insights.claim: Screvala notes that many 'new economy' companies that were Davids three years ago — Zomato, Nykaa, Lenskart — have themselves become Goliaths, complicating the small-brand-advantage narrative.
insights.speaker: Ronnie Screwvala
insights.so_what: Reminds investors that incumbency in Indian consumer tech turns over fast; today's challenger brands can scale to dominance within a few years.
insights.quote: many of them have become Goliaths and they were David's about three years back

insights.claim: A key Foundery consumer trend is Indians experimenting with multiple cuisines, creating demand for India-made condiments, dips and sauces as Western/Eastern recipes get adapted into Indian versions.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: Points to a specific white-space — locally manufactured premium condiments/ingredients — driven by cuisine diversification among affluent Indians.
insights.quote: Indians are now willing to experiment with multiple cuisines. So we need a lot of ingredients for that cuisines and India can't import everything

insights.claim: A second Foundery bet is 'better-for-you' / health products with no maida, less sugar and healthier ingredients, citing a protein product with 28g protein and 150 calories priced at 500 rupees as a category template.
insights.speaker: KB / Nikhil Kamath
insights.so_what: Identifies premium health-positioned FMCG as a core category, with a concrete price/spec point investors can benchmark margins and willingness-to-pay against.
insights.quote: 28 grams of protein, 150 calories cost 500 rupees

insights.claim: Screvala argues India's startup ecosystem is over-indexed on tech and that balancing it with a consumption-story focus on food, health and pets is a healthier, under-served allocation.
insights.speaker: Ronnie Screwvala
insights.so_what: A contrarian capital-allocation call to shift attention from tech to consumer products; signals where these operators see neglected opportunity.
insights.quote: everyone is so over-indexed on tech, I think the fact that we're talking about an India consumption story... it's a good balanced way

insights.claim: KB argues that brand adoption curves in India have compressed dramatically — what once took years (e.g. Haldiram) now goes zero-to-100 much faster across all categories.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: Faster adoption curves shorten the window to scale a brand but also shorten its defensibility; affects expected hold periods and competitive churn.
insights.quote: Today to zero to 100 is much faster than it was ever

insights.claim: KB argues that consumer loyalty among the new generation is much lower due to media saturation, meaning brands must be reimagined and pivoted to stay relevant rather than expecting 20-30 year lifespans.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: Lower loyalty and faster change argue for a portfolio of evolving brands over single long-duration franchises — directly supporting the multi-brand studio structure.
insights.quote: The loyalty factor of this generation is much, much lower. Because the kind of content and media they get to consume is far, far more

insights.claim: The founders aspire to build Indian consumer brands that sell not only domestically but in the US and other countries, treating the US as the largest consumer market and an export aspiration.
insights.speaker: Ronnie Screwvala / Nikhil Kamath
insights.so_what: Signals export ambition for Foundery brands; the upside case depends on whether India-origin premium brands can travel to Western markets.
insights.quote: the aspiration for all of us is to see Indian brands, which not only do well in India, but they sell in the US or in other countries

insights.claim: The Foundery's core methodology is that no business starts product-first — they build consumer-first, then brand and design, treating the specific product as almost secondary to category and theme.
insights.speaker: KB / Santosh
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model: enter via consumer insight and category gap, then fit product in — relevant for evaluating how Foundery brands are positioned versus product-led competitors.
insights.quote: no businesses start with brand first. Nobody. We strongly believe it is brand, design and consumer first. Consumer, brand and design first.

insights.claim: The Foundery deliberately frames businesses as categories/themes rather than products — e.g. 'building in water' instead of 'a swimwear brand' — to find category gaps before designing the product.
insights.speaker: Nikhil Kamath
insights.so_what: A theme-first canvas approach that lets a brand expand across products; signals the brands are being built for category ownership, not single SKUs.
insights.quote: you won't say, we want to build a swimwear brand. You'll say, we want to build in water

insights.claim: The Foundery's idea-generation is rooted in extensive proprietary consumer research built over years, which Santosh says gives them a better 'nose' for opportunities than others.
insights.speaker: Santosh
insights.so_what: The claimed edge is proprietary ground-level consumer insight; if real, it is the studio's key defensible asset and the basis for higher hit rates.
insights.quote: we do this extensive amount of consumer work... there is a better nose, if you like, for opportunities

insights.claim: KB says he has created 120+ businesses and considers the Foundery uniquely un-pivoted — its model has not changed significantly from conception to execution, unlike his other ventures.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: Establishes the operator's track-record credibility (120+ businesses) and signals unusual conviction stability in the Foundery concept.
insights.quote: I've created 120+ businesses and I don't think so there is anything like Foundery

insights.claim: The Foundery's design relied on a deliberately multidisciplinary team — life/leadership coaching, AI+FMCG, design/pedagogy, education, brand and younger-generation voices — which KB credits as the rare ingredient he cannot replicate elsewhere.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: Suggests the studio's edge is organizational/multidisciplinary, not just capital — harder for a pure-capital competitor to copy.
insights.quote: how can a multidisciplinary approach take... I'm trying this creation method in other areas, it just doesn't work

insights.claim: The founders argue traditional paid celebrity endorsement no longer works with consumers; instead they embed the celebrity's real personality and story into the brand as a co-founder with equity.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: Signals a structural shift in influencer/celebrity marketing toward equity-aligned co-creation; relevant for valuing celebrity-led D2C brands.
insights.quote: consumer is not looking at endorsement brands now anymore. Anybody who comes in, takes money and endorses a brand, I don't think so that works anymore

insights.claim: KB claims paid celebrity endorsement still works in B2B because dealers and distributors are reassured by a famous face, even though it no longer moves end consumers directly.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: A nuanced distinction — celebrity equity for consumer trust, paid endorsement for channel/distribution — useful for evaluating where endorsement spend still pays off.
insights.quote: But I'm told that still works in B2B. The dealers, distributors still are okay because you are using some celebrity

insights.claim: The team is building celebrity-led brands including Super You and Flux with Ranveer Singh and Momcare with Deepika, treating the celebrities as genuine co-founders with skin in the game rather than barter endorsers.
insights.speaker: KB / Nikhil Kamath
insights.so_what: Concrete examples of the equity-cofounder model in market; their performance is a real-world test of whether genuine celebrity alignment outperforms endorsement.
insights.quote: Super You with Ranveer, Flux with Ranveer, a bunch of others. I think Deepika, Momcare.

themes.theme: Abundance vs scarcity philosophy of building

insights.claim: KB's founding thesis is that abundance — giving founders everything (capital, mentors, manufacturers, ecosystem) — can create great companies, deliberately removing the struggle most Indian founders treat as essential.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: The whole Foundery is a live experiment testing whether resourced founders outperform struggle-forged ones; the result informs how studios should be capitalized.
insights.quote: Ronny believes in constraint and I believe in abundance. And I believe abundance can create something.

insights.claim: Screvala embodies the opposing scarcity/frugality philosophy, attributing his own success to thinking big at scale but executing frugally, shaped by a lower-middle-class background and low entitlement in his first 20 years.
insights.speaker: Ronnie Screwvala
insights.so_what: The scarcity counter-thesis is a hedge inside the model; investors can watch whether abundance-built brands burn capital faster than frugal ones.
insights.quote: thinking big and thinking at scale and being obsessed with that has seen me through a lot... But doing it with frugality

insights.claim: Kamath frames Foundery's premise bluntly: 'The street believes struggle makes the founder. We just removed the struggle' — explicitly questioning whether a head start makes or unmakes a founder.
insights.speaker: Nikhil Kamath
insights.so_what: States the central unresolved question of the model; the cohort's outcomes are the evidence on whether removing adversity helps or hurts founder formation.
insights.quote: The street believes struggle makes the founder. We just removed the struggle.

insights.claim: Foundery is built on two stated design principles — abundance (holding nothing back) and giving (transferring wisdom, ideas, capital and ecosystem rather than extracting from founders), which KB says shifted founders from suspicion to trust.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: The 'giving' framing is how the studio justifies its large equity stake and builds founder loyalty; central to whether the cohort stays committed long-term.
insights.quote: Foundery has been built on two design principles. One is abundance... And second is the whole concept of giving.

insights.claim: A cohort founder argues abundance is not actually abundant for society, expressing insecurity that India is moving away from broad-based abundance in the near term despite the affluence in the room.
insights.speaker: guest (cohort founder)
insights.so_what: A sober counterpoint that the premium-consumer thesis is built on a narrow affluent base; a risk flag if India One growth stalls.
insights.quote: abundance is not abundant. Abundance is abundant for a few people sitting in this room. For most of society, it is not.

themes.theme: India One vs India Two & social mobility

insights.claim: Kamath argues that if you believe in capitalism's trickle-down, building India One export brands creates upstream demand (e.g. an Udaipur ingredient supplier) that lifts India Two, and that India One itself will double in 4-5 years.
insights.speaker: Nikhil Kamath
insights.so_what: The growth case for premium brands rests on India One doubling and demand trickling up/down; a key assumption underpinning the addressable-market expansion.
insights.quote: This India won is gonna grow... by the time you get into real business in four, five years, that India won will double

insights.claim: KB holds that genuine social mobility — India Two moving to India One — can only happen through education, even as he simultaneously bets on the Foundery's non-education entrepreneurship model.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: Reveals a tension in the operators' worldview between entrepreneurship-as-engine and education-as-engine for mobility; affects how broadly the consumption thesis can scale.
insights.quote: the India 2 coming to India 1 can only happen through education

insights.claim: Cohort members acknowledge the program selects from a privileged pool — 99% speak good English, many returned from the US, and most don't worry about monthly income — raising the risk that India Two-style founders are systematically excluded.
insights.speaker: guest (cohort founder)
insights.so_what: A selection-bias risk: brands designed by India One insiders may misjudge broader markets, and the 'self-made' narrative is largely absent in the cohort.
insights.quote: 99% of the people speak good English. They come from families that allow them to take the risk, or they don't have to worry about their monthly salary

insights.claim: KB argues a critical and hard skill is India Two operators understanding the India One consumer, and that the program's unlearning process is meant to bridge that gap before brands are built.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: Frames consumer-empathy across India's income tiers as the core competency the program must instill; a failure point if the unlearning doesn't take.
insights.quote: India 2 people understanding India 1 consumer, not so easy

insights.claim: KB reframes failure as non-binary in consumer business — unlike cinema's first-day binary verdict, consumer brands get continuous feedback and chances to improve, so the only real failure would be not continuing.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: Reframes downside risk for consumer brands as iterative rather than terminal, supporting the patient-capital, keep-going posture across the portfolio.
insights.quote: consumer business is not like cinema where first day, first show, you are gone... There's no binary outcome here.

insights.claim: Screvala stresses founders must commit to the 91st day and beyond as a permanent long-term commitment, warning that treating the 90-day demo day as the finish line makes the venture purely transactional.
insights.speaker: Ronnie Screwvala
insights.so_what: Sets the expectation that fundraising at demo day is optional, not a milestone of honor — a deliberate counter to startup status-chasing that shapes founder incentives.
insights.quote: what happens after the 90th day is the 91st day... after the 90th day comes the 91st day

insights.claim: KB acknowledges that competitive founders and the market will treat whoever raises money at the highest valuation on demo day as the winner, even though he argues valuation isn't the right measure of business quality.
insights.speaker: KB (Kishore Biyani)
insights.so_what: Flags that valuation-as-status will drive founder behavior at demo day regardless of the operators' philosophy — relevant to how aggressively cohort brands will price rounds.
insights.quote: Anybody who is raising money at a higher value is getting judged. Being a unicorn is a status symbol.

insights.claim: Kamath candidly describes wealth, fame and capital as drugs requiring ever-larger doses, framing his own motivation for the Foundery as chasing successive highs rather than a single goal.
insights.speaker: Nikhil Kamath
insights.so_what: Insight into the lead backer's personal incentives; useful context for judging the durability of his commitment to recurring Foundery batches.
insights.quote: All of the things that you mention are like drugs. The more you consume of a drug, the more drugs you need to get high. So I'm on that path.

insights.claim: Screvala argues unpredictability is a prized leadership virtue — initially crediting Trump for shaking up an over-settled world before saying the approach later went too far into pure national self-interest.
insights.speaker: Ronnie Screwvala
insights.so_what: A mental model on leadership and unpredictability that informs how this operator may run businesses and negotiate; the self-interest critique also signals his geopolitics read.
insights.quote: unpredictability is the core for leadership... If you're a little unpredictable, everyone's around on their toes in a good way

insights.claim: The founders are already planning a second Foundery batch next year and envision it as a recurring annual IP, meaning equity, brand and reputation decisions in batch one set precedents for multiple future cohorts.
insights.speaker: KB / Bilal
insights.so_what: Recurrence compounds the model's funnel and brand value but also raises the stakes on batch-one outcomes, which become a five-year public reference point.
insights.quote: I think we are thinking of a batch next year already

insights.claim: Screvala defines the only true failure scenario as discontinuing the program — even if all 20 brands generate no sales, he argues the other parts are fixable because consumer business allows iteration.
insights.speaker: Ronnie Screwvala
insights.so_what: Establishes continuation as the operators' key commitment and the real risk to monitor; capital and reputation are durable only if the program persists across batches.
insights.quote: the only real failure would be if we don't continue because the other parts are all fixable

insights.claim: The founders expect every business to follow its own trajectory and want some founders to fail without a safety net, explicitly rejecting the idea of catching every cohort member when they fall.
insights.speaker: Ronnie Screwvala
insights.so_what: Signals a portfolio mindset that tolerates individual failures for cohort-level learning — relevant to expected dispersion of outcomes across the 20 brands.
insights.quote: sometimes we'll want them to fall. Because that's the best way that it's going to work there.

insights.claim: The cohort skews younger and more female than male, with founders aged 20 to 37, which KB and Screvala highlight as a deliberate and unusual bet on backing very young Indian entrepreneurs.
insights.speaker: Ronnie Screwvala
insights.so_what: Demographic skew toward young women founders differentiates the cohort and ties to the women-led-GDP thesis; affects the kinds of brands and risk profiles produced.
insights.quote: We've got more women entrepreneurs than men entrepreneurs here
